purpose: Strong action verbs for resume bullet points, grouped by function. Use these to lead every bullet point. Rotate verbs — never repeat the same verb more than twice on a resume.
usage: Pick the verb that most accurately describes the action. Avoid overused verbs: managed, led, worked on, helped, responsible for.

leadership_and_management.description: Used for people management, team direction, organizational influence

building_and_creating.description: Used for creating products, features, systems, or content from scratch

improving_and_optimizing.description: Used for making existing systems, processes, or outcomes better

analyzing_and_researching.description: Used for data analysis, research, investigation, and insights work

communicating_and_influencing.description: Used for presentations, stakeholder management, writing, and cross-functional alignment

growing_and_scaling.description: Used for revenue growth, user growth, market expansion, and scaling operations

delivering_and_executing.description: Used for project delivery, execution, operations, and shipping on time

problem_solving.description: Used for diagnosing issues, debugging, troubleshooting, and finding solutions

training_and_enabling.description: Used for teaching, onboarding, knowledge transfer, and documentation

verbs_to_avoid.reason: These verbs are weak, passive, or overused — they describe duties rather than achievements.

bullet_point_rules.framework: CAR (Challenge → Action → Result) or XYZ (Accomplished X as measured by Y by doing Z)
bullet_point_rules.must_include: At least one metric per bullet (%, $, time, headcount, scale)
bullet_point_rules.verb_placement: Action verb is always the FIRST word of the bullet
bullet_point_rules.tense: Past tense for all previous roles; present tense for current role only
bullet_point_rules.length: Maximum 2 lines per bullet — if longer, split into two bullets
bullet_point_rules.specificity: Name the tool, technology, team size, or business unit — never stay generic
